title: The Crucifixion and History: How Strong Is the Evidence?
subtitle: A methodological audit of what historians can and cannot establish about the death of Jesus
summary: Mainstream historians often call the crucifixion of Jesus the single most secure fact about his life, citing multiple early attestation and the criterion of embarrassment. A close methodological audit shows that this attestation is less independent than it is popularly presented, that several specific narrative details are genuinely contested even among committed Christian scholars, and that both the mainstream and the Quranic positions ultimately rest, at their edges, on trust in a source beyond ordinary historical verification. This article lays out the consensus case, the critical audit, and the Quran's account side by side, without declaring a winner.

seo.metaTitle: The Crucifixion and History: How Strong Is the Evidence?
seo.metaDescription: A deep-study audit of the historical case for Jesus' crucifixion — Ehrman, Sanders, and Crossan's consensus, the Q source, Licona's Matthew 27 controversy, trial difficulties, and the Quran's account in 4:157-158, presented fairly on both sides.

sections.title: The Short Answer
sections.body:
How solid is the historical evidence that Jesus was crucified? Among professional historians of the first century — including scholars with no religious stake in the outcome — the crucifixion is often named as the single most secure fact about Jesus' life, more certain than almost anything else reported about him. This companion piece to "The Crucifixion Question" does not repeat that article's comparison of the Christian, historical, and Quranic positions. Instead it audits the historical case itself: what "multiple attestation" actually rests on, how independent the sources really are, where the passion narrative shows signs of growth over time, and how specialists in Gospel dating, source criticism, and Roman administration weigh the specific details.

The honest answer has two layers. At the level most people encounter it — a headline claim that "historians agree Jesus was crucified" — the case is about as strong as historical claims about the ancient world get. At the level of a methodological audit — tracing each source to its origin, testing narrative details against external evidence, and asking what the earliest writer, Paul, does and does not say — the case rests on fewer independent legs than the headline suggests, and several specific details (the earthquake and walking dead in Matthew, the night trial, the Barabbas custom) are contested even among Christian scholars. This article walks through both layers, then sets the Quran's account of the same event, and the argument some Muslims build on it, alongside them without declaring a winner.

sections.title: Introduction: An Audit, Not a Prosecution
sections.body:
This is a deep-study companion to "The Crucifixion Question," which introduces the basic disagreement between Christianity and Islam and states plainly that mainstream historians regard the crucifixion as historically well attested. That article was honest as far as it went. This one goes further: it opens the toolbox historians actually use — multiple attestation, the criterion of embarrassment, source independence, redaction criticism — and applies it with the rigor a specialist would, including to claims that are popularly treated as settled but that specialists themselves debate.

The method here is an audit, not a prosecution or a defense of either faith. The first section states the consensus case in the words and reasoning of its own leading proponents — Bart Ehrman, E. P. Sanders, John Dominic Crossan — because an honest audit must first state what it is auditing at full strength. The second section subjects that case to the same tools of source criticism mainstream scholarship itself developed, following named scholars into territory where they disagree with one another, marking clearly which positions are majority views and which are minority arguments. A third section examines a single Hebrew psalm some Muslim writers cite in support of their case, alongside how psalms scholars actually read it. A fourth sets out the Quranic position and the strongest mainstream response to it. A final section states plainly what can and cannot be concluded, and where the real, remaining disagreement lies.

sections.title: What Historical Method Can and Cannot Settle
sections.body:
Before weighing evidence, it helps to be explicit about the tool doing the weighing. Historical-critical method, as practiced in university history and religious-studies departments, works by inference to the most probable explanation of surviving evidence: it asks which reconstruction of the past best accounts for the documents, artifacts, and silences we actually have, using ordinary criteria such as multiple independent attestation, coherence with the known context, and the unlikelihood that early communities invented material embarrassing to their own cause.

Two limits follow directly from that definition. First, the method is agnostic about the supernatural by design, not by hostility: it can conclude that early Christians came to believe Jesus had been raised, but it has no tool for certifying that a resurrection actually occurred, because a resurrection is not the kind of repeatable, comparable event the method is built to adjudicate. Second, and less often stated, the method treats a claim of miraculous intervention in an otherwise ordinary event the same way. If the Quran's claim is that God made Jesus' crucifixion appear to happen when it did not, historical method has no way to distinguish "the crowd crucified Jesus" from "the crowd was made, mistakenly, to believe it had crucified Jesus," because both scenarios would leave an identical documentary trail: communities of onlookers convinced they had witnessed a crucifixion, and a movement built afterward on that conviction. This is not a weakness invented for this article; it is why historians of every religious background restrict their conclusions to what the sources report people believed and did, and are far more careful about claims that would require verifying an undetectable fact about the world rather than a public, observable one.

With that boundary marked, the rest of this article asks a narrower and answerable question: how good, in ordinary historical terms, is the case that a man named Jesus was in fact crucified by Roman authority under Pontius Pilate — not whether he rose, and not whether, as the Quran claims, appearances deceived those present.

sections.title: The Consensus Case: “The Most Secure Fact We Have”
sections.body:
Ask almost any specialist in first-century history — Christian, Jewish, agnostic, or atheist — which single fact about Jesus of Nazareth is most secure, and crucifixion under Pontius Pilate is the answer given more often than any other. John Dominic Crossan, a historical-Jesus scholar and former Catholic priest who does not accept a bodily resurrection, put it as plainly as the discipline allows: "That he was crucified is as sure as anything historical can ever be" (Jesus: A Revolutionary Biography, 1994). E. P. Sanders, in his influential Jesus and Judaism (1985), opened his study with a short list of what he called "almost indisputable facts" about Jesus — among them, that Jesus was crucified outside Jerusalem by the Roman authorities. Bart Ehrman, in Did Jesus Exist? (2012), argues the same case in different words: the crucifixion is attested about as widely and as early as anything in the Jesus tradition, and the criterion of embarrassment cuts hard in its favor, since a movement built around a crucified would-be king had every reason to suppress that detail and essentially no reason to invent it.

The case these scholars build rests on three legs. The first is multiple, reasonably independent attestation. Paul, writing in the early 50s CE — within about two decades of the event and earlier than any surviving Gospel — already assumes the crucifixion as settled fact requiring no defense (the next section examines his earliest surviving statement of it). Mark, generally dated to the years around and after 70 CE, narrates it as the climax of his Gospel. Two non-Christian sources add independent external voices. Josephus, a Jewish priest-historian with no stake in Christian claims, records in his Antiquities of the Jews (Book 18) that Pilate, "at the suggestion of the principal men amongst us," condemned Jesus to the cross — a passage most specialists judge to preserve a genuine Josephan core once several confessional phrases inserted by later Christian copyists are set aside, as this library's companion article on Josephus and the external evidence for Jesus examines in full. Tacitus, a Roman senator with no Christian sympathies, writing around 115-120 CE, records that "Christus... suffered the extreme penalty during the reign of Tiberius at the hands of one of our procurators, Pontius Pilatus" (Annals 15.44, Church and Brodribb translation) — independent Roman testimony, even though it was written some eighty-five years after the event, probably drawing on what Christians themselves were saying by that time rather than on an independent Roman archive.

The second leg is the criterion of embarrassment applied to the mode of death itself, not merely to its occurrence. Crucifixion was, in the Roman world, a punishment reserved for slaves, rebels, and the lowest social classes — a public, prolonged, deliberately shaming form of execution. A movement proclaiming its founder as Israel's promised king and God's anointed had every reason to suppress or soften so humiliating a detail, and essentially no motive to invent it. That the earliest Christian preaching foregrounds the cross rather than concealing it is, on this reasoning, itself evidence that the community was working around a known and inconvenient fact rather than inventing a convenient one.

The third leg is contextual plausibility: crucifixion for sedition or claiming kingship was routine Roman practice in first-century Judea, and an itinerant preacher gathering crowds, entering Jerusalem to public acclaim, and disrupting Temple commerce during a politically volatile pilgrimage festival fits the profile of someone a Roman prefect would execute by exactly this method, on exactly the charge the Gospels report: "the King of the Jews."

Taken together, these three lines of reasoning are why the crucifixion sits on the short list of claims about Jesus that draw agreement across the ideological range of the discipline — evangelical, mainline, Jewish, and secular scholars converge here in a way they do not on almost any other question about him.

sections.title: “A Stumbling Block”: Paul's Earliest Word on the Cross
sections.body:
The clearest textual expression of the embarrassment argument comes from Paul's own pen, in a letter to the church at Corinth usually dated to the early-to-mid 50s CE — earlier than any surviving Gospel, and only about two decades after the events themselves. Paul writes:

"but we preach Christ crucified; a stumbling block to Jews, and foolishness to Greeks," (1 Corinthians 1:23, World English Bible)

Paul is not softening the scandal; he is naming it in order to build a theological argument out of it. To a first-century Jewish audience, a crucified Messiah collided head-on with the assumption that God's anointed king would be vindicated and exalted, not put to death by a pagan power in the manner reserved for slaves and rebels. To a Greco-Roman audience, crucifixion was a mark of the lowest social shame, making devotion to a crucified man an object of ridicule rather than reverence. Paul's own word for this is skandalon — the stumbling block a modern reader recognizes in "scandal."

Historians who are not themselves Christian point to exactly this passage as strong evidence that the earliest Christian movement was not free to invent whatever origin story it liked; it was working, within living memory of the event, around a specific, painful, and well-known fact that it could not erase and instead had to reinterpret. That the reinterpretation succeeded theologically does not, on this reasoning, change what it was reinterpreting.

sections.title: The Critical Audit, Part One: How Independent Are the Sources, Really?
sections.body:
The consensus case leans heavily on the phrase "multiple, independent attestation." A careful audit has to ask how independent the attestation actually is, because the answer changes how much weight the multiplicity can bear.

Start with the Gospels themselves. This library's companion article on Gospel authorship and dating sets out the mainstream position in full: none of the four canonical Gospels names its own author inside the text, the traditional attributions were affixed by the church a generation or more after composition, and the majority scholarly position — "Markan priority" — holds that Mark was written first (generally dated to the years around 70 CE) and that the authors of Matthew and Luke each had a copy of Mark in front of them and built much of their own passion narratives directly on it, supplementing it with additional material of their own. On this widely held reconstruction, Matthew and Luke are not two further independent witnesses to the crucifixion; for the passion narrative in particular, they are redactions of Mark's account with local additions and omissions layered on top. John's Gospel, generally dated to around 90-100 CE, is usually treated as more independent of the Synoptic tradition in its wording, though scholars disagree about how much of that independence reflects separate historical tradition rather than later theological development.

What this means for the crucifixion specifically: the claim that "the Gospels multiply attest the crucifixion" is true in the sense that all four books report it, but it overstates the stronger claim some popular presentations imply — that four separate, independently sourced eyewitness traditions converge on it. On the majority scholarly reconstruction, what we more precisely have is one earliest narrative (Mark, itself already some forty years removed from the event, and of unknown, non-eyewitness authorship) plus two derivative expansions of it (Matthew, Luke) plus one later and theologically distinct account (John). Paul's letters, predating all four Gospels, are the genuinely independent witness in the strict sense — but Paul, writing to already-established churches about a shared and settled belief, supplies almost no narrative detail about the crucifixion itself: no named Sanhedrin members, no Barabbas, no specific hour, no earthquake. He confirms that a crucifixion under Roman authority happened and stood at the center of the earliest preaching; he does not corroborate any of the specific staging details found only in the later Gospel narratives.

None of this overturns the consensus case for the bare fact of a crucifixion — Paul's early, independent, unadorned attestation of an execution the movement had to explain rather than invent remains real evidence, as does the material in Josephus and Tacitus. What a careful audit shows is that the popular image of four unrelated Gospel writers and two Roman-era historians all separately confirming a single, detailed narrative overstates the actual shape of the evidence: strip away literary dependence, and the genuinely independent voices for the passion narrative's specific contents shrink to a handful, several of them brief, none of them by a claimed eyewitness.

sections.title: The Critical Audit, Part Two: What the Q Source Does — and Does Not — Say
sections.body:
A second, less commonly discussed piece of source evidence deepens the audit. Alongside Mark, most specialists in the Synoptic Problem posit a second source behind Matthew and Luke: a hypothetical collection of Jesus' sayings that both writers appear to draw on independently of Mark, conventionally labeled Q (from the German Quelle, "source"). It is important to be exact about Q's evidentiary status before using it for anything: no manuscript of Q has ever been found. It is a scholarly reconstruction inferred from material Matthew and Luke share but Mark does not, and while the two-source hypothesis (Mark plus Q) commands wide assent among specialists, it is not universal — a significant minority, following the older Farrer hypothesis, argue instead that Luke used Matthew directly and that no separate sayings-source ever existed. Everything that follows about Q should be read with that hypothetical status in view.

With that caveat stated, the content scholars reconstruct as Q is striking for what it lacks. John S. Kloppenborg, whose Formation of Q (1987) and later Q, the Earliest Gospel (2008) shape most current discussion, describes Q as consisting almost entirely of sayings of Jesus, with no birth narrative and — the point that matters here — no passion or resurrection narrative at all. Kloppenborg's influential stratigraphic analysis proposes an earliest sapiential layer of wisdom speeches urging what he describes as a countercultural lifestyle of love of enemies, nonretaliation, debt forgiveness, and reliance on a provident God, with a later layer of apocalyptic judgment sayings inserted afterward. On this reconstruction, the earliest recoverable stratum of Jesus tradition available to Matthew and Luke beyond Mark centers on Jesus as a prophetic sage summoning Israel back to a demanding, Torah-rooted ethic — not on his death or resurrection at all.

Some comparative and Muslim writers have drawn attention to how closely this reconstructed portrait — a reforming teacher operating within, not against, the framework of Jewish law, calling his hearers to a purer obedience to it — resembles the Quran's own description of Jesus' mission: "And [I have come] confirming what was before me of the Torah and to make lawful for you some of what was forbidden to you..." (Quran 3:50). Both pictures center Jesus as a Torah-affirming reformer rather than as a divine figure whose death accomplishes atonement.

This comparison deserves to be stated carefully rather than overstated. Q scholarship is not in conversation with the Quran, and Kloppenborg himself draws no such comparison; the resemblance is an observation made from outside the discipline, applied to a hypothetical reconstruction whose own contents remain debated even among specialists who accept Q's existence. It also runs into a genuine complication from within Q scholarship itself: a single, much-discussed saying reconstructed for Q, paralleled in Matthew and Luke and sometimes called the "Johannine thunderbolt" for its resemblance to the elevated Christology of John's Gospel, has Jesus speak of an exclusive, revelatory relationship with God as Father. Scholars disagree sharply about how to weigh this saying: some treat it as a later insertion into an otherwise low-Christology sayings collection; others argue the sharp line between a "merely sapiential" Q and a "high" Johannine Christology has been overdrawn, and that an elevated self-understanding was present even in the earliest reconstructable stratum. Readers should treat the Q-Quran resemblance, then, as a real and interesting observation about one influential reconstruction of one hypothetical source — not as settled New Testament scholarship converging on a Quranic Christology, and not as immune to complication from within the very source it draws on.

sections.title: The Critical Audit, Part Three: A Growing Story, Mark to Matthew to John
sections.body:
A third strand of the audit concerns how the passion and resurrection narratives change shape across the four Gospels, in the order most scholars believe they were written. Historians across the religious spectrum — not only skeptical ones — have long noted a pattern of accumulating detail and heightening supernatural drama as the tradition moves from Mark, to Matthew and Luke, to John.

Mark's account is the starkest: an execution, a torn temple curtain, a centurion's confession, and — in the Gospel's earliest and best-attested ending — an empty tomb discovered by frightened women, with no resurrection appearance narrated at all:

"They went out, and fled from the tomb, for trembling and astonishment had come on them. They said nothing to anyone; for they were afraid." (Mark 16:8, World English Bible)

The longer ending found in most modern Bibles (Mark 16:9-20) is judged by the great majority of textual critics to be a later addition absent from the earliest and best manuscripts, a question this library's article on textual variants treats in full. Matthew, writing after Mark and drawing on it, adds an earthquake at the crucifixion itself, a second earthquake at the tomb with an angel who rolls back the stone in view of a guard found in no other Gospel, and this striking passage:

"Behold, the veil of the temple was torn in two from the top to the bottom. The earth quaked and the rocks were split. The tombs were opened, and many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep were raised; and coming out of the tombs after his resurrection, they entered into the holy city and appeared to many." (Matthew 27:51-53, World English Bible)

This is, on any reading, an extraordinary claim: a general resurrection of "many" dead saints, walking into first-century Jerusalem and being seen by its residents. It appears nowhere else — not in Mark, Luke, or John; not in Paul's letters, where a mass resurrection of the righteous dead would be exactly the kind of triumphant vindication his argument in 1 Corinthians 15 could have used; and not in any surviving Jewish or Roman source from a city whose population, on Matthew's own account, would have witnessed walking corpses in its streets during one of its most crowded festival weeks. Josephus, whose Antiquities and Jewish War record public disturbances in Jerusalem in exhaustive detail, says nothing of it.

The scholarly response to this specific passage is itself a genuine and revealing controversy, because it does not split neatly along a Christian-versus-skeptic line. Michael R. Licona, an evangelical New Testament scholar whose 700-page The Resurrection of Jesus: A New Historiographical Approach (2010) is a sustained defense of the historicity of Jesus' bodily resurrection, nonetheless concluded that this "strange little text" reads as "special effects" belonging to the conventions of ancient apocalyptic writing rather than as a report of a literal event, proposing that "it seems best to regard this difficult text in Matthew a poetic device added to communicate that the Son of God had died and that impending judgment awaited Israel" (The Resurrection of Jesus, pp. 548-553). The reaction from fellow evangelicals was immediate and sharp: Norman Geisler publicly called on Licona to recant, a critique circulated on Geisler's website arguing that treating any resurrection narrative as a non-literal "poetic device" was self-defeating for a book built to defend the historicity of the greater resurrection, and Al Mohler, president of the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary, wrote that "dehistoricizing this text is calamitous and inconsistent with the affirmation of biblical inerrancy." Under this pressure, Licona resigned his positions as research professor at Southern Evangelical Seminary and as apologetics coordinator for the North American Mission Board on October 4, 2011 — not because a skeptical scholar had challenged the passage's historicity, but because a defender of the resurrection had.

The controversy matters for this audit for a precise reason: it shows that the judgment "Matthew 27:51-53 is probably not a report of a literal event" is not confined to scholars hostile to Christian claims. It is held by at least some committed defenders of the resurrection itself, who read the passage as vivid apocalyptic imagery meant to signal a cosmically significant death, rather than as journalism. Other serious Christian scholars maintain the passage should be read as straightforward historical narrative and reject the genre argument entirely; the point is not that one side has won, but that the passage sits inside live, unresolved disagreement among people equally committed to defending the crucifixion and resurrection as historical events. Luke and John, for their part, contain no earthquake and no walking saints; Luke's resurrection appearances instead expand on Mark's spare account with substantial new material, and John adds further material still, each Gospel's resurrection account growing progressively richer in circumstantial, apologetically pointed detail than the one before it. Defenders of each Gospel's reliability note that additional, non-contradictory detail is not automatically evidence of legendary invention — later writers may simply have had access to further authentic tradition Mark chose to omit — and that explanation remains live throughout. The audit's finding is narrower: the trajectory from spare to elaborate is real and undisputed as a literary pattern; what causes it — access to more genuine tradition, or accumulating embellishment — is exactly the question on which scholars, including committed Christian ones, disagree.

sections.title: The Critical Audit, Part Four: A Minority Thesis — Mark and Homer
sections.body:
One further argument belongs in a full audit precisely because it illustrates how a genuinely minority position gets weighed and aired: Dennis R. MacDonald's claim, developed at length in The Homeric Epics and the Gospel of Mark (2000), that the author of Mark's Gospel composed several of its most memorable scenes — the calming of the storm, the feeding miracles, elements of the passion and empty-tomb narrative — as a deliberate prose reworking of scenes from Homer's Iliad and Odyssey, casting Jesus as a suffering hero who surpasses Odysseus and Achilles rather than as a straightforward historical report.

This thesis has real defenders and has generated serious academic discussion, but it is, by any reasonable count, a minority position within Markan scholarship, and it has met pointed, detailed criticism. Robert Rabel, reviewing the book for the Bryn Mawr Classical Review (2000), credited MacDonald with some genuinely persuasive individual comparisons but concluded the work as a whole engages in "analytical overkill," pairing strong parallels with weak and implausible ones — comparing, for instance, Jesus walking on water to Hermes flying through the air — and relying on what Rabel called "the most procrustean and reductive methods of interpretation." Rabel further observed that comparably dense webs of parallels can be found between the Iliad and modern, wholly unrelated works, suggesting the pervasiveness of certain narrative patterns across cultures rather than deliberate imitation, and concluded that readers would likely find "less here than meets the eye." Other reviewers have raised similar concerns about the selectivity of the comparisons and the difficulty of establishing that resemblance implies dependence rather than coincidence or shared storytelling convention.

The reason to include a minority thesis like this one in an honest audit is not that it should be believed, but that intellectual honesty requires naming it as exactly what it is: a serious, published, peer-reviewed academic argument that most specialists in the field do not accept — rather than either suppressing it, as though no scholar takes source-critical questions about Mark's literary artistry seriously, or promoting it as though it enjoyed anything like the standing of Markan priority or the Q hypothesis. It does not affect the case for the bare historicity of the crucifixion one way or the other; it bears only on how much weight to place on Mark's specific narrative details as straightforward reportage versus deliberate literary construction — a question the criterion-of-embarrassment and multiple-attestation arguments above do not, by themselves, resolve.

sections.title: The Critical Audit, Part Five: Trial Details Historians Find Hard to Square
sections.body:
A further set of arguments concerns specific procedural and characterization details in the trial narratives, each of which mainstream scholarship treats as a genuine difficulty, alongside standard responses.

The nighttime Sanhedrin session. Mark and Matthew depict the Jewish council convening at night to try Jesus on a capital charge, on the eve of a major festival. The Mishnah — the foundational rabbinic law code, compiled around 200 CE — states in tractate Sanhedrin that capital cases must be tried and their verdicts reached during daylight hours, with a mandated second day's deliberation before a guilty verdict on a capital charge could be finalized, and it prohibits holding such trials on the eve of a Sabbath or festival (Mishnah Sanhedrin 4:1). Mark and Matthew's account, taken at face value, appears to conflict with several of these rules at once. Scholars such as Helen K. Bond have argued the Synoptic trial narratives read more naturally as reflecting later Christian apologetic concerns — shifting responsibility toward Jewish leadership and away from Rome — than as a transcript of formal legal proceedings, and Luke and John's own accounts diverge from Mark and Matthew, describing a more informal morning interrogation rather than a formal nighttime trial, which itself signals uncertainty in the earliest tradition about exactly what occurred. The standard rebuttal is chronological, and it is a genuine one: the Mishnah was codified more than a century after the crucifixion by rabbis working after the Temple's destruction, once the Sanhedrin's own authority and composition had changed beyond recognition, so it cannot simply be read backward as binding first-century procedure; some scholars argue the pre-70 Sanhedrin, operating under Roman oversight and Sadducee-priestly leadership rather than the later Pharisaic-rabbinic model the Mishnah describes, may have followed different and less formal rules entirely, in which case there may be no anachronism to explain at all.

The Barabbas custom. All four Gospels report that Pilate had a custom of releasing one prisoner to the crowd at the Passover festival, and that the crowd chose to free a rebel named Barabbas over Jesus. No Roman legal source, no Jewish source such as Josephus or Philo, and no administrative record anywhere else in the ancient world attests such a custom, in Judea or in any other Roman province. Bart Ehrman and other scholars have argued this detail looks like a Markan literary invention, designed to dramatize a contrast between an innocent Jesus and a guilty insurgent and to shift responsibility for Jesus' death toward a Jewish crowd and away from the Roman prefect who alone held the authority to crucify. More recent scholarship revisiting Roman provincial legal practice — a 2025 study by John Curran on the privilegium paschale, for instance — has argued that some form of gubernatorial clemency offered at a public festival is not implausible given what is known of Roman provincial administration more broadly, even without a directly named parallel, though this remains a minority attempt to rehabilitate a detail most specialists still regard as one of the least secure in the passion narrative.

Pilate's characterization. The Gospels portray Pontius Pilate as reluctant to condemn Jesus, searching for a way to release him, and finally washing his hands of the decision under pressure from a crowd and its leaders. This sits uneasily beside the portrait of the same man in the two substantial non-Christian sources that describe him. Philo of Alexandria, in his Embassy to Gaius, describes Pilate's administration as marked by venality, violence, thefts, assaults, abusive behavior, executions of untried prisoners, and savage ferocity, calling the man himself inflexible, stubborn, and cruel. Josephus similarly records Pilate provoking Jewish crowds — bringing military standards bearing the emperor's image into Jerusalem against Jewish religious sensibility, and violently suppressing a protest over his use of Temple funds to build an aqueduct — episodes that show a governor unconcerned with local religious feeling and willing to use force rather than negotiate it. Helen K. Bond's Pontius Pilate in History and Interpretation (1998), the standard scholarly study of the question, treats all of these portraits — Philo's, Josephus's, and each evangelist's — as rhetorical constructions shaped by their authors' own purposes rather than as neutral windows onto the historical Pilate, which cuts in more than one direction: it means the Gospels' portrayal cannot simply be dismissed as fabrication on the strength of Philo and Josephus alone, since Philo was writing a polemical appeal to the emperor Caligula with every interest in making Pilate look as villainous as possible, and Josephus wrote for a Roman audience after a catastrophic war, with his own reasons for particular emphases. A further standard response notes the two pictures are not strictly incompatible: a governor capable of brutal crowd suppression in matters of Roman authority and religious symbol might still behave more cautiously in one specific case involving factional pressure from the Jerusalem elite and an accusation he judged, on the Gospels' own account, to be religiously rather than politically motivated. Whether that reconciliation is persuasive, or whether it is special pleading to save an already-strained portrait, is left, honestly, to the reader; specialists are divided.

sections.title: The Critical Audit, Part Six: Paul's Silence on the Empty Tomb
sections.body:
The earliest and most-quoted resurrection text in the New Testament belongs to Paul, writing to the Corinthian church probably in the early-to-mid 50s CE, in a passage widely regarded by scholars across the theological spectrum as quoting a creedal formula older than the letter itself, possibly reaching back to within a few years of the crucifixion:

"For I delivered to you first of all that which I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve. Then he appeared to over five hundred brothers at once, most of whom remain until now, but some have also fallen asleep. Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles, and last of all, as to the child born at the wrong time, he appeared to me also." (1 Corinthians 15:3-8, World English Bible)

What this passage offers is a list of resurrection appearances, remarkable for its early date and its naming of still-living witnesses Paul's readers could, in principle, go and question. What it does not mention is an empty tomb, a stone rolled away, or women as first witnesses — the narrative center of all four Gospel resurrection accounts.

Two readings of that silence exist, and a fair audit states both. The skeptical reading, associated with scholars including John Dominic Crossan, holds that the empty-tomb story is a later narrative development, absent from the earliest layer of tradition available to Paul, and that Paul's language of being "raised" describes a transformation he understood in more spiritual or visionary terms than the increasingly physical later Gospel narratives describe — pointing to Paul's own subsequent argument in the same letter that the resurrection body is a changed, "spiritual body," not simply the resuscitated corpse of the earlier one. On this reading, the empty tomb entered the tradition after Paul, as Mark's community, or an earlier source Mark used, developed a narrative frame for a belief that began as appearance experiences rather than as a discovered vacancy.

The majority historical-Jesus and mainstream Christian reading pushes back on several fronts. It notes that Paul is transmitting a compressed creedal summary for a specific rhetorical purpose — establishing a chain of witnesses to defend the reality of resurrection against Corinthian doubters — not a full narrative, so its silences cannot simply be read as denials; ancient creeds of this kind routinely omit narrative detail everyone already knew. It further argues that for a first-century Jew, the vocabulary Paul uses for being "raised" inherently implies a bodily event leaving behind an empty grave, since Second Temple Judaism had no real conceptual category for a dead body being "raised" while its corpse remained interred — so an empty tomb is arguably presupposed by the vocabulary even where it is not separately narrated. And it points out that "buried... raised" already forms a before-and-after pair naturally read as physically continuous.

Neither side of this argument can be settled by the text alone; the disagreement turns on how ancient Jewish resurrection-vocabulary is best reconstructed and on how much can be inferred from silence in a creedal summary, and specialists divide accordingly. What is not in dispute is the plain fact the audit set out to establish: the earliest written resurrection testimony available to us, earlier than any Gospel, is a list of appearances with no tomb narrative attached to it, and every full narrative account of an empty tomb comes from documents written at least a decade, and probably several decades, after Paul wrote this passage.

sections.title: Psalm 20:6 and a Muslim Argument From the Hebrew Psalter
sections.body:
A less commonly discussed argument, occasionally raised in Muslim writing on the crucifixion, draws on a single verse from the Psalms rather than from the New Testament or the Quran directly:

"Now I know that Yahweh saves his anointed. He will answer him from his holy heaven, with the saving strength of his right hand." (Psalms 20:6, World English Bible)

The Hebrew word rendered "anointed" is mashiach — the same root that gives Hebrew, and eventually English, the word "Messiah." The argument some Muslim writers draw from the verse runs as follows: if God's own scripture states that God "saves" His anointed one, then a crucified, executed Messiah stands in tension with the verse's own claim. God's pattern, on this reading, is rescue rather than abandonment to a shameful death, which fits more naturally with the Quran's account that God raised Jesus to Himself (Quran 4:158) than with a Messiah left to die at his enemies' hands.

Mainstream Jewish and Christian exegesis of Psalm 20 reads the psalm differently, and it is worth stating that reading in full. Psalm 20 is classified by essentially all psalms scholars as a royal psalm — very likely a liturgical prayer offered on behalf of the reigning king of Israel or Judah before he went out to battle, asking God to grant him victory; the surrounding verses ask God to remember the king's offerings and accept his sacrifice, language fitting a pre-battle ritual rather than a claim about a Messiah who would appear many centuries later. On this standard reading, "anointed" (mashiach) in Psalm 20:6 means simply the currently reigning, divinely appointed king — every Israelite and Judahite monarch was "anointed" in this ordinary, non-messianic sense — and the psalm's confidence that God "saves" him refers to the everyday hope of victory in a specific military campaign, not a christological claim about the eventual fate of the Messiah. Notably, Psalm 20 is not among the psalms the New Testament itself connects to Jesus' death; unlike Psalm 22, which the Gospel crucifixion narratives explicitly echo, or Psalm 110, which the Gospels and Paul repeatedly apply to Jesus' exaltation, Psalm 20 appears nowhere in the New Testament's own citation of messianic proof-texts.

A fair statement of both sides, then, looks like this: the Muslim argument from Psalm 20:6 depends on reading mashiach in a later, singular, technical sense that the psalm's own historical setting does not obviously require and that the New Testament authors themselves do not apply to this particular verse; the mainstream response is not that the verse is irrelevant to messianic hope in general, but that this specific psalm, in its most probable original setting, is not making the kind of claim the argument needs it to make. Readers drawing conclusions from a single verse of a well-studied genre should weigh its original setting seriously before extending it to a promise about a crucifixion many centuries later.

sections.title: The Quranic Position, and the Case Built Upon It
sections.body:
The Quran states its own position on the crucifixion directly, in a passage rebuking those who claimed to have killed Jesus:

"And [for] their saying, "Indeed, we have killed the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, the messenger of Allah." And they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them. And indeed, those who differ over it are in doubt about it. They have no knowledge of it except the following of assumption. And they did not kill him, for certain." (Quran 4:157, Saheeh International)

"Rather, Allah raised him to Himself. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise." (Quran 4:158, Saheeh International)

The verse denies two specific things — that Jesus was killed, and that he was crucified — and gives one explanation for the gap between that denial and what onlookers believed: shubbiha lahum, "[another] was made to resemble him to them," or more literally, it was made to appear so to them. It further asserts, strikingly, that even those present were themselves "in doubt," following "assumption" rather than certain knowledge. This library's companion article, "The Crucifixion Question," sets out the range of classical Muslim interpretation of this claim — substitution theories naming various figures, and non-substitution readings that place the weight on confused appearance rather than a physical stand-in — and states plainly that no specific mechanism is required belief; this article does not repeat that ground.

Two further arguments are sometimes built on this passage and deserve to be stated in full. The first is comparative. This library's article on the Quran's use of malik ("king") for the ruler of Youssef's (Joseph's) Egypt, versus fir'awn ("Pharaoh") for the ruler of Moses' era, documents an argument that the Quran's terminology tracks a genuine historical distinction between Egypt's Middle and New Kingdom titulature — evidence, on this argument, that the Quran tends to refine the received biblical account toward greater historical plausibility rather than simply repeating it. Some writers extend this pattern to the crucifixion: if the Quran shows a tendency to adjust inherited biblical narrative toward what careful historical inquiry would support, denying the crucifixion might be read as a further instance of the same pattern. This argument should be weighed with real caution, for a reason that companion article itself supplies in full: the malik/pharaoh case it documents is heavily contested even on its own terms, including the direct objection that the Bible's own use of "Pharaoh" for the same period would, by the argument's own logic, be equally anachronistic — undercutting the claim that the terminology reflects genuine historical precision rather than available narrative convention. A pattern that is itself disputed among specialists offers a weaker foundation for extending the same reasoning to a second, doctrinally far more consequential case.

That caution matters even more here because of a further, important disanalogy. The Exodus and Joseph narratives are, as that companion article states plainly, regarded by mainstream Egyptology and biblical archaeology as largely unattested or historically unverifiable outside the Bible itself — there is real room for a "more historically plausible" correction because independent history is genuinely thin on the ground there. The crucifixion is the opposite case. As the consensus section of this article states, mainstream historians across the discipline treat the bare fact of a crucifixion under Pilate as about as historically secure as any claim about the ancient world gets. A "correction toward plausibility" argument works best where the received account was historically shaky to begin with; applying it to the single best-attested event in the entire Jesus tradition asks the argument to do more work than the pharaoh/malik case, on that article's own telling, can bear even there.

The second argument is about motive: why would a prophet claiming to correct earlier revelation invent a denial of a fact that both Jews (who claimed credit for the execution) and Christians (who built their faith upon it) affirmed, gaining nothing worldly by the denial and risking easy contradiction by anyone who had lived through the events or spoken with those who had? Muslims argue this absence of any evident worldly motive is itself evidence that the denial was received as revelation rather than composed to serve Muhammad's interests.

The mainstream and Christian response to substitutionism deserves to be stated at full strength, because it answers both the theological and the historical versions of the argument. Theologically, some specialists in early Christianity and Quranic studies — Gabriel Said Reynolds among them — have argued that Quran 4:157 need not be addressing, or correcting, mainstream Nicene Christian belief in the crucifixion at all; docetic and substitutionist Christologies, in which Jesus only appeared to suffer, or in which another figure was crucified in his place, had circulated for centuries among various Christian and gnostic groups well before Islam — the second-century heresiologist Irenaeus, for instance, reports such a substitution belief among followers of the teacher Basilides — in a religious environment where multiple competing Christologies were in circulation. If so, a denial of the crucifixion did not require Muhammad to contradict a fact universally agreed upon by all Christians everywhere; it may instead draw on an existing minority current within the very diverse Christian world of late antiquity, which would answer the "no motive to invent" argument by suggesting no invention, in the strong sense, was required at all — only the adoption of one strand already present in the region.

Historically, the response returns to the consensus case stated at the start of this article: on the majority scholarly reconstruction, the crucifixion is not a contested or historically thin claim in need of correction, but the best-attested single fact in the entire Jesus tradition, resting on early, in-principle-independent testimony from Paul, a Roman method of execution that fits the political context precisely, and a criterion of embarrassment that runs strongly against invention. On this view, the Quranic denial is not correcting a shaky historical record toward plausibility, as arguably happens with the Joseph and Exodus narratives, but overriding the strongest historical claim available about Jesus' life on the authority of revelation alone. Christian scholars are careful to note this is not, by itself, a disproof of the Quran's claim — a genuine miracle of appearance, by definition, would leave exactly this kind of overwhelming ordinary evidence undisturbed — but they hold that anyone weighing the historical evidence on its own terms, independent of which revelation one trusts, will find it points toward an actual crucifixion rather than away from one.

sections.title: Verdict: What the Audit Actually Shows
sections.body:
Two honest statements can, and should, stand side by side, because both survive the audit above.

The first: mainstream historical scholarship, across its full ideological range, including scholars with no religious stake whatsoever in Christianity's truth, affirms that Jesus of Nazareth was crucified under the authority of Pontius Pilate. This is not a fringe or lightly held conclusion; it sits closer to bedrock consensus than almost any other specific claim made about his life, resting on early attestation in Paul, narrative attestation in Mark, external corroboration in a probable Josephan core and in Tacitus, and a criterion of embarrassment that runs hard against invention.

The second: a methodologically rigorous audit of the same evidence shows it to be thinner, more interdependent, and more internally contested than the popular one-line summary — "historians agree Jesus was crucified" — suggests. The Gospels' "multiple attestation" is substantially literary dependence on Mark rather than four separate eyewitness traditions; the earliest written resurrection testimony, Paul's, narrates appearances without any empty-tomb story; a specific and dramatic Matthean detail, the earthquake and walking saints, is judged non-historical apocalyptic imagery by scholars on both the skeptical and the committed-evangelical side of the aisle, at real professional cost to at least one of the latter; a named minority of scholars argue that parts of Mark's narrative art draw on Homer rather than on eyewitness memory; and several procedural details in the trial narratives — the nighttime Sanhedrin session, the otherwise unattested Barabbas custom, and Pilate's uncharacteristically indecisive Gospel portrayal against his character in Philo and Josephus — sit in genuine, unresolved tension with what is known from outside the Gospels, with serious arguments and serious rebuttals on both sides of each.

These two statements are not a contradiction; they answer different questions. "Did a crucifixion event, involving a man named Jesus, occur under Pilate?" is answered by the overwhelming majority of historians in the affirmative, and this article's audit has found no grounds to overturn that majority judgment. "Is the full, detailed narrative texture of the passion and resurrection story, as popularly summarized, as secure in every particular as the bare fact of an execution?" is a different and harder question, and the honest answer, on the evidence surveyed here, is no: several specific details are contested even among believing Christian scholars, and the source-independence behind "multiple attestation" is weaker than it is often presented as being.

Within that honestly stated picture, the Quran's claim that the man on the cross was not, in fact, Jesus is a coherent minority historical position, exactly to the extent that any claim of undetected substitution beneath a well-attested public event can be coherent: it does not require denying any specific piece of evidence surveyed above, because a genuine act of divine deception in appearance, by its own logic, would leave behind exactly the documentary record historians actually have. It requires, instead, trusting a source outside the ordinary evidentiary chain — revelation — to override what that evidentiary chain, read on its own terms, points toward. That is, in its structure, the same move Christian faith makes at its own decisive point, trusting apostolic testimony and creedal formula to affirm a resurrection that historical method cannot itself certify.

The majority position among professional historians remains what it has long been: Jesus was crucified. This article's audit does not overturn that. What it offers instead is the state of play in full — where the case is genuinely as strong as its proponents say, where it is weaker and more contested than popular summaries suggest, and where, on both the Christian and the Islamic side, the argument finally passes from the historian's tools into the hands of faith. Readers are left, as with the companion article this one extends, to weigh that transition for themselves.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Is it true that historians consider the crucifixion "the most certain fact" about Jesus?
A: In a specific sense, yes. Scholars across the ideological range — from evangelical Christians to secular historians such as Bart Ehrman and John Dominic Crossan — regularly cite it as among the best-attested facts about his life, resting on early attestation in Paul, narrative attestation in the Gospels, external corroboration in Josephus and Tacitus, and the criterion of embarrassment. This article's audit does not overturn that consensus regarding the bare fact of an execution; it examines how much weight the specific narrative details built on top of that bare fact can bear.

Q: Doesn't "multiple attestation" from four Gospels plus two Roman-era historians settle the matter?
A: The attestation is real but less independent than it first appears. Mainstream source criticism holds that Matthew and Luke drew directly on Mark for much of the passion narrative (Markan priority), so three of the four Gospels are not fully separate witnesses; Josephus's passage is judged by most scholars to contain a genuine core beneath later Christian editing; and Tacitus wrote roughly eighty-five years after the event. Paul remains the earliest and most independent witness, though he supplies almost no narrative detail.

Q: Did Michael Licona deny that Jesus was crucified or resurrected?
A: No. Licona's 2010 book is a sustained historical defense of the resurrection. The controversy concerned a single, separate detail — Matthew's report of an earthquake and risen saints walking into Jerusalem (Matthew 27:51-53) — which Licona argued should be read as apocalyptic imagery rather than a literal event. He maintained his position on the resurrection itself throughout the dispute, but the backlash led him to resign his positions at Southern Evangelical Seminary and the North American Mission Board in 2011.

Q: Does Paul's silence about an empty tomb mean the story was invented later?
A: Scholars disagree. Some argue Paul's list of resurrection appearances in 1 Corinthians 15:3-8, with no mention of an empty tomb, reflects an earlier form of the tradition before the tomb narrative developed. Others argue that first-century Jewish vocabulary for being "raised" already implies a vacated grave, and that a compressed creedal summary naturally omits details its first audience already knew. The text itself does not settle which reading is correct.

Q: What does Quran 4:157 actually claim happened?
A: It denies that Jesus was killed or crucified and states that "it was made to appear so" to those present, adding that even they were "in doubt" and following "assumption" rather than certain knowledge. It does not specify a mechanism; classical Muslim commentary offers various proposals, including substitution or confused appearance alone, and none is required belief.

Q: Is the Quran's account simply the same as earlier Christian docetist beliefs that Jesus only appeared to suffer?
A: Not quite, though some scholars see a family resemblance worth noting. Classical docetism held that Jesus lacked a real physical body altogether, which the Quran does not teach — it affirms Jesus was fully human, born, eating, and praying. Some scholars, such as Gabriel Said Reynolds, argue Quran 4:157 may instead be engaging a range of substitutionist Christologies, distinct from full docetism, already circulating among some Christian groups in late antiquity, rather than inventing an unprecedented claim.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body:
The works below approach this question from several angles: a leading non-Christian historian's case for the crucifixion's historicity; the primary source for the Matthew 27 controversy discussed above, written by a scholar defending the resurrection; the standard scholarly study of how ancient authors portrayed Pontius Pilate; and a study of the history of Muslim interpretation of Quran 4:157.

As with every article in this library, the list exists so readers can check the claims for themselves against primary scholarship rather than take this article's summary on faith. All quoted scripture in this article was fetched live from api.alquran.cloud (Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation) and bible-api.com (World English Bible), and reproduced verbatim; quotations from modern scholars were checked against the cited works and, where available online, the original reviews and press coverage of the controversies described.

quranVerses.arabic: وَمُصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَلِأُحِلَّ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَجِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍۢ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ
quranVerses.translation: And [I have come] confirming what was before me of the Torah and to make lawful for you some of what was forbidden to you. And I have come to you with a sign from your Lord, so fear Allah and obey me.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَقَوْلِهِمْ إِنَّا قَتَلْنَا ٱلْمَسِيحَ عِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ لَفِى شَكٍّۢ مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِلَّا ٱتِّبَاعَ ٱلظَّنِّ ۚ وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ يَقِينًۢا
quranVerses.translation: And [for] their saying, "Indeed, we have killed the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, the messenger of Allah." And they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but [another] was made to resemble him to them. And indeed, those who differ over it are in doubt about it. They have no knowledge of it except the following of assumption. And they did not kill him, for certain.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: بَل رَّفَعَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Rather, Allah raised him to Himself. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: but we preach Christ crucified; a stumbling block to Jews, and foolishness to Greeks,
They went out, and fled from the tomb, for trembling and astonishment had come on them. They said nothing to anyone; for they were afraid.

Behold, the veil of the temple was torn in two from the top to the bottom. The earth quaked and the rocks were split. The tombs were opened, and many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep were raised; and coming out of the tombs after his resurrection, they entered into the holy city and appeared to many.

For I delivered to you first of all that which I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve. Then he appeared to over five hundred brothers at once, most of whom remain until now, but some have also fallen asleep. Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles, and last of all, as to the child born at the wrong time, he appeared to me also.
Now I know that Yahweh saves his anointed. He will answer him from his holy heaven, with the saving strength of his right hand.

furtherReading.title: Did Jesus Exist? The Historical Argument for Jesus of Nazareth
furtherReading.author: Bart D. Ehrman
furtherReading.note: A prominent non-Christian historian's full case for the historicity of Jesus and his crucifixion, arguing the consensus case this article opens with.

furtherReading.title: The Resurrection of Jesus: A New Historiographical Approach
furtherReading.author: Michael R. Licona
furtherReading.note: The primary source for the Matthew 27:51-53 controversy discussed in this article, written by an evangelical scholar defending the resurrection's historicity.

furtherReading.title: Pontius Pilate in History and Interpretation
furtherReading.author: Helen K. Bond
furtherReading.note: The standard scholarly study comparing Pilate's portrayal in Philo, Josephus, and the four Gospels, and what each reveals about its author's purposes.

furtherReading.title: The Crucifixion and the Qur'an: A Study in the History of Muslim Thought
furtherReading.author: Todd Lawson
furtherReading.note: A scholarly survey of how Muslim commentators across the centuries have interpreted Quran 4:157, from substitution theories to readings centered on appearance alone.
